(May 15, 1918[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin50s-1) – May 8, 2008) was an American [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") singer. He was a [Nashville sound](/wiki/Nashville_sound "Nashville sound") (country/popular music) innovator of the late 1950s, and scored 147 songs on the *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_Magazine "Billboard Magazine")* country music charts, second only to [George Jones](/wiki/George_Jones "George Jones"). He sold more than 85 million records. A member of the [Grand Ole Opry](/wiki/Grand_Ole_Opry "Grand Ole Opry") (beginning 1943) and the [Country Music Hall of Fame](/wiki/Country_Music_Hall_of_Fame "Country Music Hall of Fame") (beginning 1966), Arnold ranked 22nd on [Country Music Television](/wiki/Country_Music_Television "Country Music Television")'s 2003 list of "The 40 Greatest Men of Country Music."

Musical artist

**Richard Edward Arnold** (May 15, 1918[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin50s-1) – May 8, 2008) was an American [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") singer. He was a [Nashville sound](/wiki/Nashville_sound "Nashville sound") (country/popular music) innovator of the late 1950s, and scored 147 songs on the *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_Magazine "Billboard Magazine")* country music charts, second only to [George Jones](/wiki/George_Jones "George Jones"). He sold more than 85 million records. A member of the [Grand Ole Opry](/wiki/Grand_Ole_Opry "Grand Ole Opry") (beginning 1943) and the [Country Music Hall of Fame](/wiki/Country_Music_Hall_of_Fame "Country Music Hall of Fame") (beginning 1966), Arnold ranked 22nd on [Country Music Television](/wiki/Country_Music_Television "Country Music Television")'s 2003 list of "The 40 Greatest Men of Country Music."

## Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eddy_Arnold&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early years")]

Arnold was born on May 15, 1918, on a farm near [Henderson, Tennessee](/wiki/Henderson,_Tennessee "Henderson, Tennessee").[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin50s-1) His father, a [sharecropper](/wiki/Sharecropping "Sharecropping"), played the [fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle"), while his mother played guitar. Arnold's father died when he was just 11, forcing him to leave school and begin helping on the family farm.[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin50s-1) This led to him later gaining his nickname, the **Tennessee Plowboy**. Arnold attended [Pinson High School](/wiki/Pinson_High_School "Pinson High School") in [Pinson, Tennessee](/wiki/Pinson,_Tennessee "Pinson, Tennessee"), where he played guitar for school functions and events. He quit before graduation to help with the farm work, but continued performing, often arriving on a mule with his guitar hung on his back. Arnold also worked part-time as an assistant at a [mortuary](/wiki/Mortuary "Mortuary").[[2]](#cite_note-cmenc-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Downtown_henderson_tennessee.jpg/250px-Downtown_henderson_tennessee.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_henderson_tennessee.jpg)

Downtown Henderson, Tennessee, the city near which Arnold was born

In 1934, at age 16, Arnold made his debut on WTJS-AM in [Jackson, Tennessee](/wiki/Jackson,_Tennessee "Jackson, Tennessee").[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin50s-1) He began performing at local nightclubs and was hired permanently by WTJS in 1937. In 1938, he was hired by WMPS-AM in [Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Memphis,_Tennessee "Memphis, Tennessee"), where he was one of its most popular performers. He soon left WMPS for KWK-AM in [St. Louis, Missouri](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri"),[[3]](#cite_note-Streissguth1997-3) followed briefly by a spot at WHAS-AM in [Louisville, Kentucky](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky").

He performed for [WSM (AM)](/wiki/WSM_(AM) "WSM (AM)") on the [Grand Ole Opry](/wiki/Grand_Ole_Opry "Grand Ole Opry") during 1943 as a solo artist.[[2]](#cite_note-cmenc-2) In 1944, Arnold signed a contract with [RCA Victor](/wiki/RCA_Victor "RCA Victor"), and with manager [Colonel Tom Parker](/wiki/Colonel_Tom_Parker "Colonel Tom Parker") who would later manage [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"). Arnold's first single was little noticed,[[4]](#cite_note-4) but the next, "[Each Minute Seems a Million Years](/wiki/Each_Minute_Seems_a_Million_Years "Each Minute Seems a Million Years")", scored number five on the country charts in 1945.[[2]](#cite_note-cmenc-2) Its success began a decade of unprecedented chart performance; Arnold's next 57 singles all ranked in the top 10 in the US country charts, including 19 number-one successes.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 1946, Arnold scored his first major success with "[That's How Much I Love You](/wiki/That%27s_How_Much_I_Love_You_(Eddy_Arnold_song) "That's How Much I Love You (Eddy Arnold song)")". In 1948, he had five successful songs on the charts simultaneously. That year, he had nine songs in the top 10; five of these were number one and scored there for 40 of the year's 52 weeks. With Parker's management, Arnold continued to dominate, with 13 of the 20 best-scoring country music songs of 1947–1948.[[2]](#cite_note-cmenc-2) He became the host of [Mutual Radio](/wiki/Mutual_Broadcasting_System "Mutual Broadcasting System")'s Purina-sponsored segment of the *Opry* and of Mutual's *Checkerboard Jamboree*, a midday program shared with [Ernest Tubb](/wiki/Ernest_Tubb "Ernest Tubb") that was broadcast from a Nashville theater.[[6]](#cite_note-tubb-6) Recorded radio programs increased Arnold's popularity, as did the [CBS Radio](/wiki/CBS_Radio "CBS Radio") series *Hometown Reunion* with the [Duke of Paducah](/wiki/Duke_of_Paducah "Duke of Paducah"). Arnold quit the Opry during 1948, and his *Hometown Reunion* briefly broadcast in competition with the Opry on Saturday nights. In 1949 and 1950, he performed in the Columbia movies *[Feudin' Rhythm](/wiki/Feudin%27_Rhythm "Feudin' Rhythm")* and *[Hoedown](/wiki/Hoedown_(film) "Hoedown (film)").*

Arnold began working for television in the early 1950s, hosting *[The Eddy Arnold Show](/wiki/The_Eddy_Arnold_Show "The Eddy Arnold Show")*.[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin50s-1) The summer program was broadcast successively by all three television networks, replacing the [Perry Como](/wiki/Perry_Como "Perry Como") and [Dinah Shore](/wiki/The_Dinah_Shore_Show "The Dinah Shore Show") programs.[[7]](#cite_note-7) He also performed as a guest and a [guest host](/wiki/Guest_host "Guest host") on the [ABC-TV](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") show *[Ozark Jubilee](/wiki/Ozark_Jubilee "Ozark Jubilee")* from 1955 to 1960.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Arnold featured in the syndicated *[Eddy Arnold Time](/wiki/Eddy_Arnold_Time "Eddy Arnold Time")* from 1955 to 1957.[[3]](#cite_note-Streissguth1997-3) From 1960 to 1961, he hosted [NBC-TV](/wiki/NBC "NBC")'s *[Today on the Farm](/wiki/Today_on_the_Farm "Today on the Farm")*.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Second career: The Nashville sound

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eddy_Arnold&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Second career: The Nashville sound")]

With the rise of [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") in the mid 1950s, Arnold's record sales began to decline, though fellow RCA Victor country recording artist [Jim Reeves](/wiki/Jim_Reeves "Jim Reeves") found a greater audience with popular-sounding string-laced arrangements. Arnold annoyed many in the country music establishment by recording with [Hugo Winterhalter](/wiki/Hugo_Winterhalter "Hugo Winterhalter") and his Orchestra at the [RCA Victor Studios](/wiki/RCA_Studios_New_York#24th_St "RCA Studios New York") in New York. Winterhalter's pop-oriented arrangements of "[The Cattle Call](/wiki/The_Cattle_Call "The Cattle Call")" and "The Richest Man (in the World)", however, helped to expand Arnold's appeal beyond its country music base.[[10]](#cite_note-encm-10) This style, pioneered by Reeves and Arnold, became known as the "[Nashville Sound](/wiki/Nashville_Sound "Nashville Sound")".[[10]](#cite_note-encm-10) During 1953, Arnold and Tom Parker had a dispute, and Arnold fired him.[[11]](#cite_note-11) From 1954 to 1963, Arnold's performances were managed by Joe Csida; in 1964, Csida was replaced by Jerry Purcell.[[12]](#cite_note-skyline-12)

Arnold embarked on a second career that brought his music to a more diverse audience. In the summer of 1965, he had his first number-one country song in 10 years, "[What's He Doing in My World](/wiki/What%27s_He_Doing_in_My_World "What's He Doing in My World")" and struck gold again six months later with the song that became his most well-known, "[Make the World Go Away](/wiki/Make_the_World_Go_Away "Make the World Go Away")",[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin50s-1) accompanied by pianist [Floyd Cramer](/wiki/Floyd_Cramer "Floyd Cramer") on piano and featuring the [Anita Kerr](/wiki/Anita_Kerr "Anita Kerr") Singers. As a result, Arnold's rendition became an international success. "Make The World Go Away" became his only top ten pop hit.[[10]](#cite_note-encm-10)

Bill Walker's orchestra arrangements provided the lush background for 16 continuous successes sung by Arnold in the late 1960s. Arnold performed with [symphony orchestras](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra") in New York City, Las Vegas, and Hollywood. He performed in [Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Carnegie_Hall "Carnegie Hall") for two concerts, and in the Coconut Grove in Los Angelos.[[10]](#cite_note-encm-10) In 1966, Arnold was inducted into the [Country Music Hall of Fame](/wiki/Country_Music_Hall_of_Fame "Country Music Hall of Fame"), the youngest performer to receive the honor.[[13]](#cite_note-country_music-13) The following year, Arnold was voted the first-ever awarded [Country Music Association](/wiki/Country_Music_Association "Country Music Association")'s Entertainer of the Year.[[14]](#cite_note-cmaawards-14) Two years later, he released an autobiography named *It's A Long Way From Chester County*.[[15]](#cite_note-autobio-15)

Having been with RCA Victor since 1944, Arnold left the label in 1973 for [MGM Records](/wiki/MGM_Records "MGM Records"), where he recorded four albums, which included several top-40 successes. He returned to RCA in 1976 and recorded for the company for the remainder of his career.[[2]](#cite_note-cmenc-2)

## Later years and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eddy_Arnold&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Later years and death")]

During the 1980s, Arnold declared himself semi-retired, but he continued recording. In 1984, the [Academy of Country Music](/wiki/Academy_of_Country_Music "Academy of Country Music") awarded Arnold its Pioneer Award. His next album, *You Don't Miss A Thing*, was not released until 1991.[[2]](#cite_note-cmenc-2) Arnold performed road tours for several more years.[[16]](#cite_note-1991return-16) By 1992, he had sold nearly 85 million records, and had a total of 145 weeks of number-one songs, more than any other singer.[[2]](#cite_note-cmenc-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Eddy_Arnold.jpg/250px-Eddy_Arnold.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eddy_Arnold.jpg)

Arnold in 1998

In 1996, RCA issued an album of Arnold's main successes since 1944 as part of its 'Essential' series.[[2]](#cite_note-cmenc-2) Arnold, then 78 years old, retired from active singing, though he still performed occasionally.[[10]](#cite_note-encm-10) On May 16, 1998, the day after his 80th birthday, he announced his final retirement during a concert at the Hotel Orleans in Las Vegas.[[17]](#cite_note-retire-17) That same year, the [National Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Recording_Arts_and_Sciences "National Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences") inducted the recording of "Make The World Go Away" into the [Grammy Hall of Fame](/wiki/Grammy_Hall_of_Fame "Grammy Hall of Fame"). In 2000, he was awarded the [National Medal of Arts](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Arts "National Medal of Arts").[[18]](#cite_note-18) In 2005, Arnold received a [Lifetime Achievement Award](/wiki/Grammy_Lifetime_Achievement_Award "Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award") from the [Recording Academy](/wiki/Recording_Academy "Recording Academy"),[[19]](#cite_note-laa-19) and later that year, released a final album for RCA titled *After All These Years*.[[20]](#cite_note-gormly-20)

Arnold died from natural causes on May 8, 2008, in a care facility in Nashville, at age 89. His wife of 66 years, Sally Gayhart Arnold, had preceded him in death by two months. They are survived by two children (Richard E. Arnold, Jr., and JoAnn Arnold Pollard), two grandchildren (K. Michelle Pollard and R. Shannon Pollard, Jr.), and four great-grandchildren (Katie E. Pollard, Jack Pollard, Rowan Pollard, and Ben Johns).[[21]](#cite_note-may8death-21)

On May 31, 2008, RCA released "To Life" as a single from the album *After All These Years*. It debuted at number 49 on the Hot Country Songs charts, Arnold's first entry in 25 years and the recording by the oldest person to chart in *Billboard* magazine. It set the record for the longest span between a first chart single and a last: 62 years and 11 months ("Each Minute Seems Like a Million Years" debuted on June 30, 1945), and extended Arnold's career chart history to seven decades.[[22]](#cite_note-arnold-22)

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eddy_Arnold&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Discography")]

For a list of singles and albums, see [Eddy Arnold discography](/wiki/Eddy_Arnold_discography "Eddy Arnold discography").

* *[Wanderin' with Eddy Arnold](/wiki/Wanderin%27_with_Eddy_Arnold "Wanderin' with Eddy Arnold")* (1955)
* *Chapel on the Hill* (1956)
* *A Little on the Lonely Side* (1957)
* *When They Were Young* (1957)
